exploration_log.aha_moment: The `discarded` enumerator is the last in the enum and is intentionally excluded from the `order` array (handled by a runtime bounds check). Using `value_t::discarded` as the expected array size gives exactly 9 — the current size — and will fail to compile if a new enumerator is inserted before `discarded`.
exploration_log.hindsight: Nothing to change; the fix was a one-liner once the enum layout was understood.

alternatives: Use a separate constexpr count of non-discarded enumerators
Use value_t::discarded as the size bound
key_decisions.reasoning: discarded is the last enumerator and is excluded from order; its numeric value equals the expected array size; this is the minimal and most self-documenting assertion

search_sequence.result: Found enum with 10 enumerators (null through discarded), order array with 9 elements, and bounds check `l_index < order.size()`; discarded at index 9 is intentionally not in the array

solve_notes: In `value_t.hpp`, the comparison operator uses a 9-element `order` array indexed by enum values. The `discarded` enumerator (last in the enum, value = 9) is intentionally excluded. I added a `static_assert(order.size() == static_cast<std::size_t>(value_t::discarded))` immediately after the array definition, which compiles to `9 == 9` today but will fail if a new enumerator is inserted before `discarded` without extending the array.
task_text:
Fix value_t ordering array not guarded by static_assert against enum changes

The `operator<` and `operator<=>` comparisons for `value_t` in
`detail/value_t.hpp` use a fixed-size `std::array<std::uint8_t, 9>`
to map each enum value to an ordering rank. The array size (9) must
match the number of non-discarded enumerators, but there is no
compile-time check enforcing this. If a new value type is added to
the `value_t` enum without extending the `order` array, the bounds
check (`l_index < order.size()`) silently treats the new type as
unordered rather than producing a compilation error. Fix
`value_t.hpp` to add a `static_assert` verifying the array size
matches the enum range.

No additional context is needed — both defs together are sufficient to understand why `value_t::discarded` is the right bound and where to place the assert.
